sentences.text: No.
Yes.
They are throwing over the wall.
We knew where.
And they were throwing over the wall.
I could be shot.
It wasn't--
Jewish police wasn't armed.
They only had a club.
But the Polish police, we had to cope with them and the Germans.
In SS uni--
In the SS uni-- the Wehrmacht was actually not allowed in the ghetto, only the SS.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And also, they only could walk in to.
The conditions were starvation, typhus.
And I'll give you a few examples.
People were laying in the street.
When they died at home, families were putting them out naked outside in order to not to report the death, so they could have their rations card.
They used the rations card.
Not report them was also [INAUDIBLE] for the building, saved the building for a quarantine.
Because right away, oh, it was typhus in here.
So they put them someplace in the ghetto outside.
It was horrible to get out in the morning and just sit and look at all those corpses sometime covered with a newspaper.
And then later on, the Germans were coming to [INAUDIBLE] and took photos.
The Jews are laying in the street like rat, like dead rats.
So, we organized.

We-- the civil disobedience group, the Jewish military organization, [INAUDIBLE] of our own and for those who passed through typhus-- so, you know that you cannot get flecked fever twice-- and carried them into the cemetery on push carts or on the box.
And I was one of them.
Yes.
But the Jewish Cemetery was actually out of the ghetto.
It was a gate.
Direct it opened and closed up the street, so you can go into the ghetto.
And then it was mass graves.
No one knew where somebody is buried.
So we got carried on our backs.
I say I was one of some of them, because I went through the typhus in summer.
It didn't take me completely.
My mother also had typhus in the ghetto, also survived it.
I thought we were carrying them soon for us was a different-- like I say, two victims in one.
But we deprived the Germans, the Nazis, of taking pictures.
And we deprived the living Jews of a horrible vision.
And also, we saved the dignity of the deads.
And the main thing, we succeeded in defeating, in a way, the Nazis, not to have them make pictures.
[NON-ENGLISH] That's how life was going on.
One day, I was walking the streets.
That's why my wife didn't want to be interviewed.
One Sunday afternoon, we're walking the ghetto.
I walked out.
And other side, a young couple, young married couple were walking together.
The wife was visible pregnant.
I stopped by two-- two SS man.
And one took out his gun and went to her.
The other one took the husband.
[INAUDIBLE] I'll give you, now, you have a speedy delivery, and put two bullets into her belly.
Poor husband fell on the ground, was crying.
[INAUDIBLE] on him.
They killed him, too.
They walked away like nothing would happen.
Blow on the ground to [INAUDIBLE] to-- to this.
So killings were a daily-- daily occurrence.
It happened on [INAUDIBLE].
I remember the address where it happened.
Did they deport people in groups?
Or, did they select a few?
Or, nothing or that sort yet?
You see they organized with the Labor Department from the-- this-- I didn't tell you anything else yet about the Jewish [NON-ENGLISH] But Mr.
[INAUDIBLE], everybody thinks he was a big hero.
But me, he was the biggest coward.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Can I go backwards a little bit with the ghetto?
With the organization of the ghetto.
They organized a Judenrat, the Jewish-- a Jewish committee, Jewish [NON-ENGLISH].
And they was that-- [INAUDIBLE] was the president-- nominate as the president of this.
Was a teacher, teach in the-- [NON-ENGLISH], professional school, and which belonged to the Jewish [NON-ENGLISH] before the war.
And they called it-- I forgot the English name for-- for professional school.
No, no, no.
It's mechanics and this--
The name will come back from [INAUDIBLE].
The [INAUDIBLE] school.
And today-- so he came one day.
The Nazis came in.
[INAUDIBLE], he was a teacher there.
He was engineer, came to-- to school.
And the Nazis took him, you will be the president of the [INAUDIBLE].
He had no stand in the community at all, was unknown.
He happened to live on the same street that we did, but [INAUDIBLE] up.
We were [INAUDIBLE].
He lived [INAUDIBLE].
Never knew him.
Well, later on, I found out where he lived.
And-- and he [INAUDIBLE] Jewish life.
I'd say he was not involved in Jewish community.
He had a job.
And that's all what it was.
Then he got to himself a few these ministers, is how I would call, in this cabinet.
And there were some, in a certain way, a little bit helping.
Not us, but helping the Nazis.
They were very good order takers in carrying them out.
And they organized a Department of Labor on the German request.
The Department of Labor had to deliver every day so many Jews to go out for day's work or for several day's work.
They went in the-- in the morning, came back in the evening.
And when they're following, they sometimes for a few days, sometimes only for one day.
If they didn't they manage to get a quota, then they started to grab from the state with the help of the Jewish police.
So by the way, the chief of police was a convert.
His original name was [?
Shenkman. ?] Then he became a high ranking officer in the Polish police, converted himself.
And his name was [?
Josef Sharinksi. ?] It was a miserable character.
You know, one convert is worse than 10 born guys.
So he organized the police.
Police was about-- I would say about 1,000 of 1,500 men.
Some of them, very honest Jewish boys.
And some used the opportunity.
I don't want to say they were collaborators.
But they used for personal gains.
And, OK, maybe they were collaborators, special the time when the final action started.
Maybe to save their own lives, they-- they were collaborators.
But the worse [INAUDIBLE] was a coward.
Because he was told the final solution camp that on June 22, the day he committed suicide, the action of resettling, so-called, the Jews from Warsaw will commence.
Instead he'll tell us [INAUDIBLE], he just shot himself, a coward's way out.
If he intended already to shot himself, he could say it.
He was a very little man.
That deviated from what I was saying before.
But it just came to me that I left this out.
No.
After the first action, we go back a little bit before we go into the first action.
We had infiltrated the Jewish military organization all positions in the police and in the people who were cleaning the gestapo headquarters.
And one day in April of 1942, we receive from our infiltrator noticed that the German requested a force of 100 policeman for midnight at-- at April 19-- April 18.
No, it was April-- April 12, I'm sorry, April 12, 1942 to be brought to the [NON-ENGLISH].
That was the gestapo headquarters in the ghetto.
On [INAUDIBLE] 103.
That's the name, the address of the street.
And the purpose is that some action has to be taken against a few hundred Jews.
By 10 o'clock at night, we received a copy of the list.
Who were they Jews?
It was only about 70 Jews on the list.
We immediately mobilized our people.
And they were going in the middle of the night, which was dangerous.
The curfew in the ghetto, not the curfew out of the city, the curfew in the ghetto, it started about 8 o'clock in the evening going and notifying the people to escape.
From the 70 some people, only 12 could not be reached.
Or, we didn't-- we couldn't reach them.
So 12 were shot [INAUDIBLE] out from the houses and shot the back of their heads in the streets.
These supposedly were Jews, which they claim were influential money-wise.
And they may help the Jewish underground.
But the gestapo [INAUDIBLE] from 70 some people, we only found people.
So the only one who could betray us was the-- the Jewish gestapo helped us.
They notified the Jewish underground.
And they went out and shot quite a number of the-- of the-- of their own people, the gestapo, the Jewish gestapo.
I'm ashamed to say it, but we had them.
Let their names be erased from Jewish memory forever.
Mandel was shot.
Alec was shot that night.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know the names.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Alec, Eichman, Alec, Mandel, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Landau.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's all what I remember at the moment.
Maybe I'll come back to it [INAUDIBLE].
I'll refresh my memory.
That was, again, a double success for us.
We deceived the German.
We saved some 60 some Jews.
And-- and first of all, they deceived the German.
And they got rid of some Jewish gestapo men.
That was still action of the civil disobedience group.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then later on, the civil disobedience group was changed to [NON-ENGLISH], avengers.
Then we were selecting our own gestapo men and liquidate them as much we could.
Occasionally, we beat them up.
And in most cases, we just eliminated them.
The first big action started July 22 of 1942.
I'll come to it.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Officially, they were transported to a to work in the fields and to work in industry and better surroundings than-- in Warsaw to work in shops.
And that was the official excuse.
As the work progressed, they were giving out two loaves of bread to volunteers and 2 kilo of marmalade.
And many hungry people came just to get the two loaves of bread and went to, so-called, [INAUDIBLE] or Trawniki, where they were supposed to work in [INAUDIBLE] and shops.
And like I said, there's the president of the Judenrat committed suicide.
But [INAUDIBLE] was going out till September, almost till September of 1942.
At the end, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] before the end of this, two man came to Warsaw whom I knew personally, [INAUDIBLE].
They contact me.
And then I took him to the Doctor-- Dr.
David [INAUDIBLE], who wrote the book I have that book.
He mention me a few times in the book.
Also, and who was in charge of the political department the Jewish military organization.
Let me just-- just step aside for one second.
If I'm talking mostly about the Jewish military organizations, in no way I want to undermine the brave people of the Jewish community organization.
They were brave boys and girls.
I talk about the Jewish military, because I was with them.
And I know the best what they were doing.
By no means I want to undermine the other group.
They were just as brave as my-- as my group, maybe even braver.
I don't know.
It cannot be measured, bravery.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The Dr.
[INAUDIBLE] was in charge of the political department, Dr.
[INAUDIBLE] and Dr.
[INAUDIBLE], who later on led the revolt in-- in this-- in Trawniki when he was caught, he and-- and David-- what is his name, I'll remind myself later-- led the revolt in Trawniki.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [INAUDIBLE] told us they escape from the transport.
And they tell us that the train master saw the train operators told them that if train is going to Treblinka then Treblinka is [INAUDIBLE].
They are being shot.
And now, they are building a crematorium.
And that, that's when the [INAUDIBLE] come out and the revenge group started.
When they told me, I believed them.
Because I knew them very well.
They were [INAUDIBLE].
They were kids with me.
That was the first information we got.
I don't know when they military-- the [NON-ENGLISH], the Jewish fighting organization got it.
You see, we could talk--
We We could talk to-- yes.
Yes, we-- we posted that information.
And we shared that [INAUDIBLE] that information with [NON-ENGLISH].
Although, that group was information only at the time.
We were organized before the whole thing started.
Well, we continued.
By the way, [NON-ENGLISH] was my [NON-ENGLISH] in [INAUDIBLE].
[NON-ENGLISH] [INAUDIBLE].
It was a small group [INAUDIBLE] block away.
And we started to organize ourself a little bit more.
I know I had day, night training sessions.

[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] At the end of that action beginning of September, all the Jews which remained are to assemble by the [NON-ENGLISH] in a, I would say, six blocks area, square area, six square areas, six square blocks.
And they were making selections.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Who could survive, hide himself?
I hid my parents, my-- my sister, my brother, my wife's mother, and her [INAUDIBLE], and her sisters.
And then after this, we went back with her mother.
I had to come special to take off where the hiding place was, with the elderly woman you could see in the picture.
But they wouldn't let her go.
And I smuggled her out with a false written-- this piece of paper signed of by Obersturmfuhrer [?
Hunt, ?] with Gothic letters.
[INAUDIBLE] more authentic.
But she's a group leader in a shop.
And she is needed to work over there.
And I brought out from her there.
[INAUDIBLE] remained still over there, didn't manage to get out or shot.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Then we had a little time till-- till January 18 of 1943.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Yeah.
That was a week after my marriage.
I married January 10.
And this was January 18.
In the ghetto?
Yes, in a private home, in my mother-in-law's apartment, which is was in the same building.
I had a chuppah.
And everything was normal, had a [NON-ENGLISH].
Give us-- they-- they put us under the chuppah and give us this-- [INAUDIBLE] blessings for the-- marriage blessings.
And according to Jewish law, you don't need a rabbi.
Any Jew can do it.
You see, so much I knew of the Jewish law.
We had quite a number of guests.
I had already people with guns staying outside and watching.
And [INAUDIBLE] was at my wedding.
That's where we-- [INAUDIBLE] was negotiating with the two groups to get together.
And he said, I would love to.
I said, blessed were the days when I was a member of [INAUDIBLE].
I learned how to hold a gun in my hand.
Now, I can teach the Jewish pacifist something.
I didn't come to anything, because of the [NON-ENGLISH], the extreme left group [INAUDIBLE] come to this.
[INAUDIBLE] who didn't want it.
And they both didn't want it.
Before that, still before September this, the-- the ghetto was cut smaller.
Then it was-- one part of the ghetto was taken out.
And that was called the small ghetto.

[PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] And we had to move from where we were to another part of the ghetto, central ghetto next to-- actually, we moved next to the headquarters of the Jewish military organization the [INAUDIBLE] 7.
And we-- and we lived in [INAUDIBLE] 5, which was very convenient for me.
Then in January 18, it was a new action.
They started-- and until today, I don't know if this was planned for three days, or just because we responded.
That was when the first shots-- Jewish shots were fired.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And that's-- [INAUDIBLE] I don't know.
After two-- two or three days, they abandoned the action.
So I am-- I am not sure.
[INAUDIBLE] thanks to us.
But in fact, maybe that because the Jewish resistance [INAUDIBLE].
And they knew that there's a strong Jewish resistance.
They call it the ghetto-- outside of the ghetto, the Nazi's called it, they are going to climb Mexico.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Because every day, another gestapo man was liquidated in a bus or by the Jewish fighting organization.
So they knew what it is.
And also, some German soldiers, some SS men, were disappearing.
Because we needed very badly the guns.
Not that we liked, we want them so much dead.
But we like-- you know, we wanted the gun.
So we invited some [INAUDIBLE] who are willing to have a drink, but only from a sealed bottle.
So we give him a sealed bottle.
So we inject it with this-- with a needle.
We injected this poison into it.
So we got-- that's where we got some good uniforms.
While we're talking about uniforms, there's-- we had the-- the shops in the ghetto was working for the Wehrmacht.
We also were stealing uniforms over there, which we had at time of the revolt we used them.
So we, yeah, we were good smugglers and good [INAUDIBLE].
We learned very fast.
And we were buying [INAUDIBLE].
We were even making American gold dollars in the ghetto, because they were [INAUDIBLE] the real American.
Because we couldn't measure so well.
So they had more pure gold.
They had-- the value was more than 22 karats of gold.
Sometimes [INAUDIBLE] is between 20 and 18.
In my wedding band, went in that [INAUDIBLE] in the pot That's why not sell it, not to buy another one.
While I was taking off, by mistake, this went in.
[PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]
--but the final.
The shops were-- the two main shops were [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE].
You probably heard the names before.
You were working in [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE], take off your shoes, make yourself--
[INAUDIBLE] called all the foreman from-- from his shops on [INAUDIBLE].
At the time, I was living already on the [INAUDIBLE].
I was transferred to the high command of the Jewish military organization to take care of the [INAUDIBLE] shops, which was normally [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE].
And I took charge of that area.
And [INAUDIBLE] called all his shop leaders for a meetings.
And he locked the doors.
[INAUDIBLE] said, tomorrow you will-- we are moving to shop peacefully to [INAUDIBLE].
You will have better quarters and everything.
But he didn't let anybody out.

So Alexander Rosenfelt, who was the editor before the war of a Jewish afternoon paper in Yiddish, [NON-ENGLISH], he was an outstanding member of [INAUDIBLE], was assigned as a [INAUDIBLE] man special to [INAUDIBLE].
And he was allowed to be at the meeting and watch the door.
But for one purpose, he went out to get [INAUDIBLE] a glass of water.
At the time, he threw down a slip of paper to his brother, this [?
Jakov ?] Rosenfelt.
And he informed us that--
